Create your story, drive your market, halberdology, or the science of using powerful words and story to drive people to take action. If you're a marketer, copywriter, or own your own business, I would like to share with you secrets that have built more wealth for more people than any other thing ever discovered. These secrets, when applied properly, are so powerful that they have never failed to increase profits, boost conversions, and create raving fans. And you will learn all these secrets for free. Throughout the course of history, a few powerful, carefully chosen words have changed everything. They have driven us to war, rallied us behind seemingly impossible feats, taught us to love, to imagine, and to create. No matter the goal, they all harness one thing, the power of persuasion. Starting today, we want to help you change your world. Master persuasion has been responsible for building some of the largest companies in the world today, and it's something almost anyone can master. I'm Kevin Halbert. My dad, Gary, created a copy and marketing dynasty and made a fortune in direct response advertising. For the first time since his death, the next generation of Halbert marketing experts will show everything our dad taught us, and everything we know about mastering the art of persuasion in the information age. What's more, we've partnered with one of the most innovative minds in marketing today, Mark McRae, to bring you a fully integrated program that will cover almost everything you may ever have wanted to know about marketing. Direct conversion, copywriting, marketing, presentation, multi and mass media markets, video sales letters, email marketing, business growth, preserving your personal and professional well-being. Of course, we will also cover the specialty that built our empire, direct response mail. We're calling it halbertology, and that's just the tip of the iceberg. How can we bring you all of that? By assembling the most prestigious group of legendary marketing professionals ever to collaborate together, all in honor of my dad, Gary. My brother Bond and I have never been as excited and humbled as we are at the outpouring of professional respect and love. This veritable who's who of copy and marketing is showing us by coming together to create this truly unique and unprecedented course. You're dying to know who they are, aren't you? I know, but here's the thing. This is so big, the presenter is so famous and sought after that I cannot share the full roster with you yet. Just know this, they are the creme de la creme, the very top, the 1% of the millionaire copywriters and marketers who names you here only in the most odd whispers. One of the first things any marketer learns is our most valuable commodity, no matter the market or the product, is time. I don't want to waste any of yours or mine, so let's get down to it, okay? In this first mini course in Habritology, I'm going to share two of the three most powerful secrets to transforming your marketing. Mark McRae's winning strategy for success in any market, his perpetual money making machine, the most important and misunderstood aspect of any marketing of any type anywhere in the world. It's not what you think. Each of those should rightly be its own full course, let alone a part of a short course. But I'm trying to give you a huge chunk of everything I've got in as condensed a manner as possible. I don't want you to miss a thing. Before we get started, let's ask you a couple of questions. Does it feel like you are never totally in control of your marketing? Are your results inconsistent? Do you wish you had an automated sales process in place that consistently made you money? Do you feel like you need help, but you don't know where to go or who to trust? I want to help you solve some of these challenges with the privileged information I learned working with one of the greatest copy graders who ever lived and some of the brightest, most talented marketers in the world today. The good news, you are about to discover what has already been proven to work through the course of many thousands of split run tests over many years costing millions of dollars in ad spend. We call it the base trilogy. And it's the foundation, the base of any great marketing. If you get this one thing right, you will go faster than you could have ever imagined. And if you get this one thing wrong, you will join the others in the graveyard of broken dreams. It's the reason why 70% of some emails are opened, while others can barely get 5% of their audience to look at their message. It's the reason why Apple has an almost cult-like following and why people will stand in the rain for 24 hours just to be the first to get their latest product. When using combination with the other things I'm going to teach you today, it could transform how you do business. You will become a magnet for your given niche and almost overnight be considered the authority in your market. As we go along, you will see how each of the three parts of the trilogy work together to create irresistible marketing. It's deceptively simple. The base trilogy consists of market, message, voice. We've boiled it down to these three words as touchstones that can act as a shorthand reminder when you're in the heat of battle crafting your winning strategy. But remember, I said deceptively simple. The three points of the base trilogy are sharp, deadly effective weapons and must be handled with utmost care. Probably by the time we're done today, you will understand how you may have been shooting yourself in the foot and how to use these powerful tools to target any customers you choose. We'll take the base trilogy in order and I will be sharing with you the insights into this killer strategy, market. We like to think of this as building your tribe where you will learn how to locate the people destined to become your lifelong raving fan customers. We are going to talk about marketing hot buttons, sex appeal and the one thing your prospects seek above all else. I want to start off by talking about an exercise my dad, Gary Halbert, used in his copywriting seminars. During these classes, one of the questions he would ask his students is, if you and I both owned a hamburger stand and we were in a contest to see who could sell the most hamburgers, what advantages would you most like to have on your side to help you win? The answers would vary. Some of the students said they would like to have the advantages of having superior meat from which to make their burgers. Others said they wanted sesame seed buns. Others mentioned location. Some usually wanted to be able to offer the lowest prices and so on. After students finished telling him what advantages they would most like to have, he would usually say something like this. Okay, I'll give you every single advantage you have asked for. I myself only want advantage and if you will give it to me, I will when it comes to selling burgers with the pants off all of you. What advantage do you want they would ask? The only advantage I want he would reply is a starving crowd. Can you think of a better demonstration of how upside down people think about markets, especially about creating one? You cannot create a market. You can only identify it. Think about it. When it comes to marketing, the most profitable habit you can cultivate is the habit of constantly being on the lookout for groups of people who have demonstrated they are starving or at least hungry for some particular product or service. The fact is markets are people. More to the point, markets are people's problems and desires. So just like you have to identify the market, you must identify your customers' problems and desires. Eugene Schwartz said it well in the opening paragraph of Breakthrough Advertising. The power, the force, the overwhelming urge to own that makes advertising work comes from the market itself and not from the copy. Copy cannot create desire for a product. It can only take the hopes, fears, and desires that already exist in the hearts of millions of people and focus those already existing desires onto a particular product. This is the copywriter's task. To create this mass desire, but to channel and direct it. What is this desire? Schwartz called it the public spread of a private want. It is the moment when a private desire is shared by a statistically significant number of people, large enough to profitably repay selling these people that a market is born. These evolving desires, based on core desires, can take years to develop and are created by social, technological, and economic forces, not by a clever ad campaign. My dad looked at opportunities differently than most marketers do. He looked for existing markets, starving crowds if you will, and then set out to find the perfect product or reinvention of a product to fill the wants of that market. However, most of you watching today likely already have an existing product or service that you want to sell. So let's talk about how you identify the hottest market for you, refine the need, and reposition your product or service to best meet the already existing desires within your market. As you will soon see, these two things are really one in the same. Because once you've really dived into the why behind your potential market, in other words, the real appeal of your product to your best prospects, the actual market will just appear before you as plain as day. Let's talk about what people really want. Who is your market and what do they want from you in order to happily give you their money? I have asked everyone from small business owners to online marketers to the CEOs of big corporations this question and seldom have I ever gotten the correct answer. The answer to that translates into you having a deep enough understanding of your prospects' dreams, desires, fears, concerns, objections, and problems to have them become not just customers but happy raving fans. Let's look at an example to help you better understand what I mean. If you are in a weight loss market, you may think your market is people who want to lose weight. That's the obvious answer, but that is just what is on the surface. Why do these people want to lose weight? Self-esteem? Are they going to dive a heart attack or get diabetes if they don't shed some pounds? Maybe their spouse is making comments that lead them to believe their relationship could be in trouble if they don't do something soon. Maybe they want to get into a relationship and feel too self-conscious to enter into one. Maybe they're just plain embarrassed when they go out in public. Maybe they want to get back to the energy and vitality they once knew. So they can do things that they enjoy but leave them winded in sweating. The point is this, markets are just feelings. Only the feelings of desire or despair. And people are all moved by their feelings regardless of how analytical and smart they would have you believe they are. Emotions drive every decision we make, every action we take, and everything we do. You believe that you justify your actions with logic, but that's just a built-in mechanism to make others see the decisions you make as smart, well-grounded, and thought out. In more simple terms, they keep us from looking foolish to others for our actions. The fact is, most of what we do is driven by fear and greed. We either want to gain pleasure or want to avoid pain. We all seek acceptance, but if you boil all human desire down to its very core, there is one thing we all seek above all else. Status. We as a species care a lot about what other people think of us. The importance we place on it is often a reflection of whose acceptance and admiration we seek, but we all want to be loved above all else. So do you know what really drives your market? Do you know their biggest hopes and dreams, their darkest fears, the problems that keep them awake at night? Take the time to find out what your market really wants, and they will gladly pay you whatever you ask for it. Markets are always people. Markets are problems. Markets are dreams and desires. Markets are hopes. Markets are fears. Markets are pain. Markets are stories. The stories people tell themselves, the ones they tell others, and the ones others tell about them, or that they can just relate to on a deep level. What's your market story? They are dying to tell it to anyone who will really listen, and they will gladly give their money to those who they feel really understand them. Everything they want stems from that story. Knowing it and understanding it is the ultimate marketing power. That brings us to the second part of the base trilogy. Message The power of words and how the right words can change the world as we know it. It is the power of the right message to the right people at the right time. The most important part of the base trilogy is the message. Get this right and people will stand in line to give you money. Get it wrong and nothing can save you from failure. We are going to get into some pretty amazing mojo that will put you miles ahead of your competition and allow you to dominate your market. The number one thing we must do is build an instant relationship with our customer. Building that relationship in the old days was about getting in the room and making a connection. The basics are still the same, but the medium has vastly changed. The room is often now a screen and the connection where prospects must be instantaneous. You can create a bond with nearly anyone with the power of words. I am going to teach you how to do just that. Words at their root are just symbols. They're really just marks on a page. What makes it a word? What makes it a specific word with a host of definition and personal thoughts attached to it is meaning. Meaning is created through shared experience and memory and is hugely linked to emotions. Here greed, lust, hunger, desire, holiness, purity, clarity, beauty. Each of these words means something and can mean different things to people in different situations. One of the most successful writers on the planet is Stephen King. In his bestselling book on writing A Memoir of the Craft, he titles a chapter What Writing is. It begins, telepathy of course. If you consider what words really do convey thoughts, emotions and meaning, he is exactly right. Do we use words to convey the thoughts and emotions we are having to our prospects? Nope. We want to use them to convey their own thoughts, emotions and meanings back to them. When you master the art of persuasion, you can tell your prospect what he thinks and he will agree with you. Can we just say, you need to bribe this widget right now and have them all line up to do so? We all know that doesn't happen. So what is missing? A compelling story that builds that instant connection between you and your product and the customer's emotions. You have to join the conversation already taking place in their head. That is what you will learn to do a bit of today. This is Maslow's Hierarchy of Needs. The base is made up of our most common needs, the fundamentals if you will, and as it progresses, the emotional impact of each need evolves. If you are a veteran marketer, this is a very familiar graphic. However, regardless of your level of marketing knowledge, the reminders contained in this one image are among the most powerful tools in your sales arsenal. It takes us from shelter, food, water, heat and safety all the way up to the nirvana of self-actualization. The deficiency needs, deneeds as defined by Maslow are, esteem, friendship and love, security, physical needs. We have found that Maslow's theory is right on. If any of the deneeds are not being met, a person feels anxious, ill-ease, angry and can even become physically ill. Meeting these physical, psychological and most important us emotional needs is essential to your ability to persuade. Why does this matter? Because at any time your prospects can be feeling any or all of these emotional needs. Whenever you are creating a marketing piece, this pyramid should be at your elbow, helping you remember why your product or service matters to your customer. Where does your product or service fit into this pyramid? That depends on a few things. Obviously, the product you are selling will determine where on the pyramid your message should focus. For example, in the case of something as basic as transportation, Volvo has sold safety for decades with great success. Ferrari is also an automobile, yet they find a great deal more success by targeting self-esteem. Both are cars, yet the messages are very different. It's all about crafting your message to the individual needs, wants or desires of your specific audience. You must meet your prospect in the emotional state they are already in and form a bond with them. Showing them how what you have is an exact match and solves their specific problem or fulfills their specific desire. The million dollar secret is to do this in such a way that they don't feel that they are being sold, but rather like they have discovered on their own the perfect product or service to fill their emotional need. To understand more clearly what they want, what they need from you, let's look again at Maslow's hierarchy of needs. It's simple really, everything falls into the pyramid at some point. If a person is starving to death, food is the only concern on his mind. As you move up the pyramid, those things towards the top only become important when the needs below them have already been met. Survival is always our biggest concern. It is instinct and controlled by our reptilian brain, the oldest portion. It is what keeps us alive. You must determine where your product or service fits in, which need it fulfills, only then can you expand on it. Let's go back to our weight loss example. Is it self-esteem? Nope, it's status. Is it that they are going to die of a heart attack or get diabetes? It's actually safety and security. Is it that their relationship could be in trouble if they don't do something soon? It's really about love and belonging. Maybe they feel too self-conscious to enter a relationship. Again it's about love and belonging. Maybe they're just plain embarrassed, only because they crave status. Maybe they want to get back the energy and vitality they once knew. They are really after safety and security. For most of us, the need of our market will be the same reason people want to win an Oscar, drive an exotic sports car, live in a mansion, or lose weight. We want to look younger, sexier, richer, healthier, and more successful. Why? Because the only thing we seek above all else after our basic survival needs have been met is status. We care most about what others think about us, how they see us. How can you refine your product or service to best meet your prospects exactly where they are right now? How can you refine your story to make it their story? The best way is to have them see you as one of them, as someone who is just like them and relates to exactly what they are feeling. That is where your story has to be carefully crafted in a way that it becomes their story, where they feel you understand their deepest, darkest desires and fears. When someone reads an advertisement, a sales letter, or an email, they are only taking the time to do so for one reason. They are seeking a solution. It may be to eliminate a pain in their life, be it physical or emotional, people will do more to remove pain than anything else. Notice I said to remove pain. You may have heard many times that people will do more to avoid pain than to gain pleasure. That's only partially true. They will do very little to proactively avoid pain, if anything at all. You never take an aspirin because you might get a headache. You take one when you have a headache. But when your head is throbbing, you will do whatever it takes to make it stop. In many cases, however, people are actually seeking pleasure of some kind. Ultimately, they seek status, love and acceptance above all else. Does your product or service target physiological needs such as food, water, shelter, clothing or sleep? Let's say it does. But even then, is that really the level of the pyramid you should be targeting? Water is a great example. Let me ask you this. If you are selling this water at $219 per bottle, what do you think you are really selling? Now you have moved up the pyramid from physiological needs to self-esteem, or more accurately, status, regardless of which physical or emotional need your product or service targets. It is still all about connecting to your prospect and being in the moment with them. So let's focus on how you do that. As I said, the first step is to join the conversation already taking place in their head. This takes a bit of detective work. You have to go where they are having these conversations and listen to what they are saying. This could be in forums, on social media, in chat rooms or surveys. The important thing is that you find where they are sharing their problems and desires with their peers, and that you pay close attention to what is being said. If you just go to your product or service along with forums, you will be shocked at just how much is being said about your niche. If there is one thing there is no shortage of, it is opinions, about any subject. David Ogilvy, one of the greatest admin of all time, was once asked, what is the most important thing for marketing to be successful? He answered, research. My dad, Gary Halbert, had this to say. I think 97% of the people who attempt to write sales letters advertise its websites and so forth do almost no research at all into their customers. And for those of you who think research is asking your customers to fill out surveys and the like, this is not really the way to do it. Why? Because when people fill out surveys or when they are in focus groups and stuff like that, they lie. Just a footnote, I don't think he meant to say people are liars. It's just that in the case of a focus group, for example, they are being paid to give you their opinion. So they often will tell you what they think you want to hear rather than the whole truth. You have got to get down amongst your potential customers and do everything you can to at least temporarily become one of them, one of that group to which you are trying to sell. Once you have a feel for what they really seek, the rest becomes easier. But there is more to it than just saying, hey, here's a solution to that problem. You have to tell your story and in doing so, make it their story. You need to tell them your story and theirs in parallel. Here's how you do that. You tell the story that they would tell about their problem or desire if they were telling it honestly, like they might to a best friend. But you make it your story. That way, they begin to connect and say, hey, they are just like me and trying to solve the same problem. You make all the ugly parts they don't want to admit about you. You become the fall guy, the scapegoat. You let them off the hook of admitting they have a need of any kind. Then you offer up the solution as something you discovered that solved your problem and let them see how it also solves theirs. John Carleton always says a story should have a setup, a plot, and a point. You want the setup to be about getting agreement that this is something of interest to their specific need or desire and that you and they are kindred spirits in this quest for answers. In Copywriting Jargon, your headline and deck are where you make this connection. It is usually most effective when written in a way where they see you as someone with the exact same problem as them. So they can nod in agreement with a sentence and say to themselves, yeah, I know exactly how you feel. I feel the same way. The plot could be the story behind how you found a solution to it. For example, how you have been searching for a solution to lose some weight and one day you were sitting in a waiting room and read how some scientists were digging up dinosaur poop in a South American jungle when they discovered a rare unknown substance that when ingested seemed to greatly boost your metabolism safely and organically and how the locals of the area were all exceptionally fit and healthy. What is important here is to make it interesting and keep it focused on them or their main interests. The point of the story is basically your call to action. If you can relate to all of what I have just said and you too want this result, then you just need to do this. How do you position your product or service so that your customers feel they must have it to meet one of their core needs? Before you can know the answer to that, you must answer these questions. What do you actually provide? A solution? Because remember what we said? Markets are problems. For them to feel okay with a decision, they have to like you. My dad Gary is famous for his newsletters. In those letters, he would share his marketing and copywriting wisdom. His theory was that likability trumps everything else, even sex appeal. How do you get customers to like you? It's easier than you think. People like people who are like them. You could tell your whole story as someone who has gone through the roller coaster of emotions attached to their problem and let the prospect nod in agreement with all of the feelings you went through and the solutions you discovered and what they meant to you. You have to boil it down to the real core emotions, to what your prospect is feeling and how what you have for them can make them feel better about themselves. Which brings us to the last part of the base trilogy. I will share with you the last and maybe the most important part of the base trilogy in my next video. Thanks for listening.
